english: col. sâb, sab, Arab. صَاحِب, formerly in India title of Moslems of high rank, now title of every European, = gentleman, sir.

also nya-sag (nya sag) Cs., 1. brawn, callosity; Sch. also: hair-side (of a skin); sag-ćan (sag can) brawny; sag-túg (sag thug) a thick brawn. -- 2. W. scale, (of a fish) nya-sag-ćan (nya sag can) scaly.
C., *sab-dár* (sab bdar) W., ysag-brdár (ysag brdar) Sch. a rasp, sag-bdár rgyag-pa (sag bdar rgyag pa) to rasp.
C. a little bubble, *sag sag zér-wa* (*sag sag zer wa*) to sparkle, to effervesce.
Pers. سقلات, 1. fine cloth, made of lé-na (le na), C. *go-nam* (*go nam*) (v. snam-bu (snam bu)). European broadcloth W. -- 2. round or twisted lace, round tape, strips of cloth set with spangles? W.

1. B., C. to-morrow, saṅ-nyiṅ (sang nyin) id. Glr.; saṅ-gi gdúgs-la (sang gi gdugs la) for to-morrow noon Dzl.; saṅ nán-par (sang nan par) Cs., saṅ sṅá-bar (sang snga bar) Glr. to-morrow morning; also absolutely: on the following day Pth., deí-saṅ (de'i sang) id.; saṅ-pód (sang pod), more frq. saṅ-lo (sang lo) next year; saṅ-pód da tsám-du (sang pod da tsam du) a year hence, this time a year. -- 2. W. particle denoting the comparative degree, inst. of las (las) or pas (pas) of B., *de saṅ i gyál-la* (*de sang i gyal la*) this is better than that. -- 3. Ld., Balt. sometimes for yaṅ (yang).
a greenish stone of which knife-handles and similar articles are said to be made W.
saṅ-ṅé (sang nge), saṅ-súṅ (sang sung) Ld. secretly, privately, whisperingly, by report, = sám-súm (sam sum).
hiding-place, chink, crevice, kyim-gyi (khyim gyi) of the house Stg., for hiding money and treasures, = sbugs (sbugs); gós-kyi (gos kyi) folds of the dress, that are a haunt of vermin.

pf. (b)saṅs ((b)sangs), fut. (b)saṅ ((b)sang), 1. to do away with, to remove (dirt etc.), to cleanse, cf. ₒtsáṅ-ba (gtsang ba), where also examples are given. -- 2. in a more gen. sense: to take away or off, kebs saṅ-ba (khebs sang ba) to uncover; *ḱo-la nyi saṅ soṅ* (*kho la nyi sang song*) C. his sleep is gone, he cannot sleep. -- 3. to spoil, to render unfit or useless, *wó-ma saṅ soṅ* (*'o ma sang song*) C. the milk is spoiled, *zem saṅ soṅ* (*zem sang song*) the cask or tub leaks C. Cf. seṅ (seng).

the Tibetan equivalent for बुद्ध; as to the etymology of the word v. sub ₒtsáṅ-ba (gtsang ba). The first historical Buddha is Saṅs-rgyás ṡā-kya túb-pa (sangs rgyas shA kya thub pa), whose family name is Gaú-ta-ma (gau ta ma) and his personal name Don-grúb (don grub), सिद्धार्थ, which, however, is not much used. In course of time several imaginary predecessors were given to him: ₒOd-srúṅ ('od srung), Gser-túb (gser thub) and ₒKor-ba-ₒjíg ('khor ba 'jig), as having existed and reigned in former periods of the world. A successor also, Byáms-pa (byams pa), was assigned to him, of whom it is supposed that he will reign at the period following this present one. According to others, however, Sākyatuḃpa (shA kya thub pa) was already the seventh Buddha that appeared on earth, the four above-named having been preceded by Tams-ćad-skyób (thams cad skyob), Gtsug-tór-ćan (gtsug tor can) and Rnam-par-gzígs (rnam par gzigs), this last one being the first of them all. These seven Buddhas then are comprised under the name of Saṅs-rgyas-rabs-bdún (sangs rgyas rabs bdun). -- But the fertile imagination of devote Buddhists has further increased the number of future Buddhas to not less than one thousand (?), appropriate names for each of them have been invented, and Prof. Schmidt has thought it worth his while, to have these thousand names reprinted in a special pamphlet. Mysticism, however, generally knows only of the five first-named Buddhas (Gautama, his three predecessors and his first successor) and to each of these five 'human' Buddhas a celestial Buddha corresponds, called 'Dhyani Buddha' or the Buddha of contemplation, whilst to every Dhyani Buddha again
